title: BAA1104: E-Portfolio and Business Analytics Challenges - Reflection
text:
Prior to this module, I had never heard of Quarto and had no idea what Git or GitHub was. To be completely honest - the first couple of weeks were tough. I found myself constantly breaking things, discovering the error and then breaking again. This process was frustrating and unrewarding at times however, I believe this is how it all clicked for me.






My best learning in this module occured when things went wrong, not when they worked first time. This trial and error approach allowed me to find the reasons why errors happen and how I could apply this going forward.

title: BAA1104: E-Portfolio and Business Analytics Challenges - Reflection
text:
My Biggest Surprise
Realising that I could build my own personal portfolio and one that I am genuinely proud of was somewhat unexpected. Having the ability to own, control and keep building on websites was something I thought only strong developers had. Despite only beginning my path in this journey, I now feel I have something valuable that represents me as an overall individual - something regular college assignments struggle to provide.

title: BAA1104: E-Portfolio and Business Analytics Challenges - Reflection
text:
The Classes
The interactive and engaging manner of classes really suited me as an analytics beginner. Trying things, making mistakes and figuring out solutions was the overall process throughout this project and I believe this was rooted in the dynamic lecture style promoting encouragement and acceptance of first-time failure. Being surrounded by students of a similar level enabled teamwork and collaborative gain within the classroom setting and made technical challenges feel less intimidating than facing them alone.

Where This Leaves Me
I’m a trainee accountant who can now build and deploy live data portfolios. This combination of financial knowledge and technical capabilities is exactly where I want to be heading. This module has pushed me further along this desired path. Continuing into the future, my portfolio presents clearly who I am, what I know and how I contribute value to others.






Going forward, I intend to keep this portfolio updated as I complete my ACCA qualification, develop new projects, and grow in my role at the Mercantile Group. Along with my LinkedIn, this will further increase my digital presence.










Note



Thank you for reading. You can explore my projects on the Projects page or get in touch if you’d like to connect.

title: Sentiment Analysis - BERT vs LLaMA 3.1
text:
This project addresses the business problem of automated sentiment classification of customer reviews across four Dublin venues within the Mercantile Group - NoLIta, Pichet, Café en Seine, and The Mercantile Hotel - using reviews sourced from OpenTable, TripAdvisor, Google, and Yelp.
Two AI models were compared:

BERT (bert-base-uncased) - a discriminative transformer model, fine-tuned on labelled review data
LLaMA 3.1 - a generative large language model, applied via zero-shot prompting with no training data







Note



This project uses real customer review data from the Mercantile Group. Formal consent was obtained from the General Manager. All personal identifiers were removed prior to analysis.

title: Sentiment Analysis - BERT vs LLaMA 3.1
text:
This project addresses the business problem of automated sentiment classification of customer reviews across four Dublin venues within the Mercantile Group - NoLIta, Pichet, Café en Seine, and The Mercantile Hotel - using reviews sourced from OpenTable, TripAdvisor, Google, and Yelp.
Two AI models were compared:

BERT (bert-base-uncased) - a discriminative transformer model, fine-tuned on labelled review data
LLaMA 3.1 - a generative large language model, applied via zero-shot prompting with no training data







Note



This project uses real customer review data from the Mercantile Group. Formal consent was obtained from the General Manager. All personal identifiers were removed prior to analysis.

title: Sentiment Analysis - BERT vs LLaMA 3.1
text:
Key Results



Model
Accuracy
F1 Score




TF-IDF + Logistic Regression (Baseline)
91.03%
0.952


BERT (Fine-tuned)
97.76%
0.987


LLaMA 3.1 (Zero-shot)
98.21%
0.990









Key finding: LLaMA 3.1 marginally outperformed fine-tuned BERT despite using zero training data - highlights the power of LLMs for domain-specific NLP tasks with limited labelled datasets.

title: Sentiment Analysis - BERT vs LLaMA 3.1
text:
Dataset

1,610 original records across 4 venues; 1,188 retained after cleaning
Binary sentiment labels: Positive (rating ≥ 4) and Negative (rating ≤ 2)
Rating 3 deliberately excluded as ambiguous
80/20 stratified train/test split
Class imbalance: ~88% positive, ~12% negative

title: Sentiment Analysis - BERT vs LLaMA 3.1
text:
Python Code - Model Comparison Visualisation

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

models   = ['TF-IDF + LR\n(Baseline)', 'BERT\n(Fine-tuned)', 'LLaMA 3.1\n(Zero-shot)']
accuracy = [91.03, 97.76, 98.21]
f1       = [95.17, 98.73, 98.98]

x      = np.arange(len(models))
width  = 0.35
colors = ['#475569', '#4C9AFF', '#A78BFA']

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5))
bars1 = ax.bar(x - width/2, accuracy, width, label='Accuracy (%)',
               color=colors, edgecolor='white', alpha=0.9)
bars2 = ax.bar(x + width/2, f1, width, label='F1 Score (%)',
               color=colors, edgecolor='white', alpha=0.5)

ax.set_ylabel('Score (%)', fontsize=11)
ax.set_title('Model Comparison — Accuracy & F1 Score', fontsize=13,
             fontweight='bold', pad=15)
ax.set_xticks(x)
ax.set_xticklabels(models, fontsize=10)
ax.set_ylim(85, 102)
ax.legend(fontsize=10)
ax.axhline(y=97, color='grey', linestyle='--', alpha=0.4, label='97% benchmark')

for bar in bars1:
    ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2, bar.get_height() + 0.2,
            f'{bar.get_height():.1f}%', ha='center', fontsize=9, fontweight='bold')
for bar in bars2:
    ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2, bar.get_height() + 0.2,
            f'{bar.get_height():.1f}%', ha='center', fontsize=9, fontweight='bold')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()




Accuracy and F1 Score comparison across three models

title: Sentiment Analysis - BERT vs LLaMA 3.1
section: Python Code - BERT Training Loss Curve
text:
Python Code - BERT Training Loss Curve

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

epochs = [1.0, 2.0, 3.0]
losses = [0.2025, 0.048, 0.016]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4))
ax.plot(epochs, losses, 'r-o', linewidth=2.5, markersize=8, label='Training Loss')
ax.fill_between(epochs, losses, alpha=0.1, color='red')
ax.set_xlabel('Epoch', fontsize=11)
ax.set_ylabel('Loss', fontsize=11)
ax.set_title('BERT Training Loss per Epoch', fontsize=13,
             fontweight='bold', pad=15)
ax.set_xticks([1, 2, 3])
ax.legend(fontsize=10)

for x, y in zip(epochs, losses):
    ax.annotate(f'{y:.4f}', (x, y), textcoords="offset points",
                xytext=(10, 5), fontsize=9, fontweight='bold')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()




BERT training loss decreasing across three epochs

title: Sentiment Analysis - BERT vs LLaMA 3.1
text:
Python Code - Sentiment Word Frequency

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

pos_words  = ['great', 'food', 'staff', 'atmosphere', 'hotel']
pos_counts = [352, 341, 281, 103, 99]
neg_words  = ['food', 'staff', 'experience', 'waited', 'service']
neg_counts = [67, 35, 24, 22, 18]

fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 4))

ax1.barh(pos_words[::-1], pos_counts[::-1], color='#34D399', edgecolor='white')
ax1.set_title('Top Positive Words', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
ax1.set_xlabel('Frequency')

ax2.barh(neg_words[::-1], neg_counts[::-1], color='#F87171', edgecolor='white')
ax2.set_title('Top Negative Words', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
ax2.set_xlabel('Frequency')

plt.suptitle('Key Sentiment Drivers — Mercantile Group Reviews',
             fontsize=13, fontweight='bold', y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()




Most frequent words driving positive and negative sentiment










Beyond sentiment classification, both models provide key insights to business operations. This NLP analysis demonstrates the capabilities both models possess to assist management on decision-making, monitoring and identifying areas of improvement across venues

title: Sentiment Analysis - BERT vs LLaMA 3.1
text:
Methodology

BERT - Discriminative Fine-tuning

Tokeniser: bert-base-uncased WordPiece tokenizer
Max sequence length: 64 tokens (justified by EDA - majority of reviews under 64 words)
Batch size: 8 (optimised for standard laptop GPU constraints)
Optimiser: AdamW, learning rate 2e-5
Training: 3 epochs with forward pass, backpropagation, and weight updates



LLaMA 3.1 - Zero-shot Prompting

Run locally via Ollama API (no cloud dependency - reduced environmental impact)
Prompt engineering to constrain output to a single word: Positive or Negative
No training data required - leverages generalised pre-trained knowledge

title: Sentiment Analysis - BERT vs LLaMA 3.1
text:
Ethical Considerations






Following the EU Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI, this project ensured:

Human oversight - model outputs are decision-support tools, not final decisions
Privacy - personal identifiers removed; formal consent obtained from Mercantile Group GM
Fairness - equal review counts taken per venue to avoid representation bias
Environmental - LLaMA run locally via Ollama to reduce cloud API carbon footprint

title: Sentiment Analysis - BERT vs LLaMA 3.1
text:
Business Implications



Scenario
Recommended Model




Limited labelled data available
LLaMA 3.1 (zero-shot)


Long-term domain-specific deployment
BERT (fine-tuned)


Quick deployment, no data science team
LLaMA 3.1


Large dataset, high accuracy priority
BERT

title: Consumer Complaint Analytics — Bank of America
text:
Bank of America processes thousands of customer complaints every year, and the traditional First-In-First-Out triage system treats every single one the same. The problem with that approach is that not all complaints are equal - some will require financial compensation, others won’t.
This BAA1108 group project replaces Bank of America’s slow reactive FIFO system into an AI-powered fast-track routing system. Using 61,022 real complaints from the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB), a Voting Classifier combining Histogram-based Gradient Boosting and Random Forest achieves 86.1% precision identifying complaints likely to require monetary relief, to be flagged to senior management before the situation escalates






🚀 Live Dashboard: analysisofcomplaints.streamlit.app

title: Consumer Complaint Analytics — Bank of America
text:
Bank of America processes thousands of customer complaints every year, and the traditional First-In-First-Out triage system treats every single one the same. The problem with that approach is that not all complaints are equal - some will require financial compensation, others won’t.
This BAA1108 group project replaces Bank of America’s slow reactive FIFO system into an AI-powered fast-track routing system. Using 61,022 real complaints from the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB), a Voting Classifier combining Histogram-based Gradient Boosting and Random Forest achieves 86.1% precision identifying complaints likely to require monetary relief, to be flagged to senior management before the situation escalates






🚀 Live Dashboard: analysisofcomplaints.streamlit.app

title: Consumer Complaint Analytics — Bank of America
text:
Key Results



Metric
Result




Model
Voting Classifier (Gradient Boosting + Random Forest)


Precision
86.1%


ROC-AUC
0.794


Accuracy
77.4%


Dataset Size
61,022 CFPB complaints


Projected Gross Savings
$510,000


Operational Cost
$39,500


Net Benefit
$470,500


ROI
1,191%

title: Consumer Complaint Analytics — Bank of America
text:
Feature Engineering
The pipeline engineered the following features from raw CFPB data:

TF-IDF vectorisation of complaint Issue text (unigrams + bigrams, 1,000 features)
Temporal features - Days_to_Forward, Month_Sin, Month_Cos, Day_of_Week, Quarter
Risk rate features - Product_Risk_Rate, State_Risk_Rate (historical payout probabilities)
Text metadata - Issue_Length, Issue_Word_Count

A few of these deserve more explanation. The cyclical sine/cosine encoding of the submission month is there to make sure the model understands that December and January are temporally close. The Product_Risk_Rate and State_Risk_Rate features embed historical payout probabilities directly into the model, giving it a baseline risk signal rooted in past performance rather than just the text of a single complaint.

title: Consumer Complaint Analytics — Bank of America
text:
Python Code - Imports & Configuration


Show imports
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

# Unified colour theme
THEME = {
    'primary': '#4C9AFF',
    'secondary': '#A78BFA',
    'success': '#34D399',
    'danger': '#F87171',
    'warning': '#FBBF24',
    'neutral': '#475569',
}
print("Libraries loaded.")


Libraries loaded.

title: Consumer Complaint Analytics — Bank of America
text:
Python Code - Model Performance Visualisation

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

metrics = ['Precision', 'ROC-AUC', 'Accuracy', 'Recall']
values  = [86.1, 79.4, 77.4, 4.6]
colors  = ['#4C9AFF', '#A78BFA', '#34D399', '#F87171']

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 4))
bars = ax.barh(metrics, values, color=colors, edgecolor='white', height=0.5)
ax.set_xlim(0, 105)
ax.set_xlabel('Score (%)', fontsize=11)
ax.set_title('Voting Classifier — Model Performance Metrics',
             fontsize=13, fontweight='bold', pad=15)
ax.axvline(x=80, color='grey', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5, label='80% benchmark')
for bar, val in zip(bars, values):
    ax.text(val + 1, bar.get_y() + bar.get_height() / 2,
            f'{val}%', va='center', fontweight='bold', fontsize=10)
ax.legend(fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()




Voting Classifier performance metrics on the CFPB test set










Precision is the standout metric here. A recall of 4.6% looks low on paper, but in the context of this system that’s intentional — the goal isn’t to catch every possible payout case, it’s to make sure that when the model does flag a complaint, it’s almost certainly a genuine financial liability.

title: Consumer Complaint Analytics — Bank of America
text:
Python Code - Financial Simulation

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Simulation parameters (from report)
COST_PER_REVIEW   = 50
AVG_PAYOUT_SAVED  = 750
flagged_cases     = 1020

thresholds    = np.arange(0.3, 0.8, 0.05)
gross_savings = [flagged_cases * AVG_PAYOUT_SAVED * (t * 0.9) for t in thresholds]
op_costs      = [flagged_cases * COST_PER_REVIEW * (1 - t * 0.3) for t in thresholds]
net_benefit   = [g - o for g, o in zip(gross_savings, op_costs)]
roi           = [(n / o) * 100 for n, o in zip(net_benefit, op_costs)]

fig, ax1 = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 4))
ax2 = ax1.twinx()

ax1.plot(thresholds, net_benefit, 'b-o', linewidth=2, label='Net Benefit ($)')
ax1.set_xlabel('Risk Threshold', fontsize=11)
ax1.set_ylabel('Net Benefit ($)', color='blue', fontsize=11)
ax1.tick_params(axis='y', labelcolor='blue')

ax2.plot(thresholds, roi, 'g--s', linewidth=2, label='ROI (%)')
ax2.set_ylabel('ROI (%)', color='green', fontsize=11)
ax2.tick_params(axis='y', labelcolor='green')

ax1.axvline(x=0.5, color='red', linestyle=':', alpha=0.6, label='Selected threshold (0.50)')
ax1.set_title('Financial Impact by Risk Threshold', fontsize=13, fontweight='bold', pad=15)
fig.legend(loc='upper right', bbox_to_anchor=(0.88, 0.88), fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()




Net benefit and ROI across different risk thresholds

title: Consumer Complaint Analytics — Bank of America
text:
Dashboard Features
The deployed Streamlit application serves three user groups:








User
View

Operational Efficiency
Processing times and workload heatmaps


Executives
Market Intelligence
Volume patterns, financial exposure, ROI tracking

title: Consumer Complaint Analytics — Bank of America
text:
Ethical Considerations







All Personally Identifiable Information (PII) was removed prior to analysis.
Sensitive demographic variables were excluded to ensure fair, bias-free predictions based solely on complaint content and product metadata (e.g race, gender, and exact location).

title: Predictive Inventory Optimisation Dashboard
text:
Note



🔄 Status: In Progress - Research Phase (Phase 2 of 4), January 2026 - August 2026.


This is a live Applied Consultancy Project at DCU, working with a 6-person team for Agvance Ltd and Animal Farmacy - a family-owned agri-nutrition wholesaler and e-commerce retailer based in Portlaoise, Co. Laois.
The client manages over 700 products across a shared warehouse, supplying farms across Ireland and the UK. Despite consistent year-on-year growth, recurring stock-outs and an experience-driven ordering process are creating operational inefficiencies and lost revenue.

title: Predictive Inventory Optimisation Dashboard
text:
Note



🔄 Status: In Progress - Research Phase (Phase 2 of 4), January 2026 - August 2026.


This is a live Applied Consultancy Project at DCU, working with a 6-person team for Agvance Ltd and Animal Farmacy - a family-owned agri-nutrition wholesaler and e-commerce retailer based in Portlaoise, Co. Laois.
The client manages over 700 products across a shared warehouse, supplying farms across Ireland and the UK. Despite consistent year-on-year growth, recurring stock-outs and an experience-driven ordering process are creating operational inefficiencies and lost revenue.

title: Predictive Inventory Optimisation Dashboard
text:
The Business Problem






Agvance and Animal Farmacy currently rely on experience-based manual reordering, resulting in regular stock imbalances, avoidable stock-outs, and inefficient working capital allocation — particularly during seasonal demand peaks.










Challenge
Impact




Experience-driven ordering
Unpredictable stock-outs


No demand forecasting system
Missed sales, customer dissatisfaction


Seasonal demand patterns
Peaks unaccounted for in reorder triggers


Multi-system data (Shopify, Turbo, MINTsoft)
No unified data view for decisions

title: Predictive Inventory Optimisation Dashboard
text:
Data Sources
Two real client datasets were used for the EDA phase:



Source
Period
Records




Turbo Inventory CSV
Jan 2022 – Mar 2025
33,213 rows


Shopify Daily Orders ODS
Jan 2025 – Apr 2026
9,700 rows

section: Python Code - Simulated Monthly Revenue Trend
text:
Python Code - Simulated Monthly Revenue Trend






The charts below use simulated data that mirrors the structure and patterns of the real client dataset. Actual figures are commercially sensitive.




import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as mticker

np.random.seed(42)

# Simulate Turbo monthly revenue (Jan 2022 – Mar 2025)
turbo_periods = pd.date_range("2022-01", periods=39, freq="MS")
turbo_base    = 45000
turbo_season  = 12000 * np.sin(2 * np.pi * (np.arange(39) - 2) / 12)
turbo_trend   = np.linspace(0, 15000, 39)
turbo_rev     = turbo_base + turbo_season + turbo_trend + np.random.normal(0, 3000, 39)

# Simulate Shopify monthly revenue (Jan 2025 – Apr 2026)
shop_periods  = pd.date_range("2025-01", periods=16, freq="MS")
shop_base     = 18000
shop_season   = 5000 * np.sin(2 * np.pi * (np.arange(16) - 2) / 12)
shop_rev      = shop_base + shop_season + np.random.normal(0, 1500, 16)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(13, 5))
ax.plot(turbo_periods, turbo_rev, color="#2196F3",
        linewidth=2, label="Turbo (2022–2025)")
ax.fill_between(turbo_periods, turbo_rev, alpha=0.1, color="#2196F3")
ax.plot(shop_periods, shop_rev, color="#4CAF50",
        linewidth=2, label="Shopify (2025–2026)")
ax.fill_between(shop_periods, shop_rev, alpha=0.1, color="#4CAF50")
ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(
    mticker.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f"€{x:,.0f}"))
ax.set_title("Monthly Revenue Trend — Turbo vs Shopify (Simulated)",
             fontweight="bold")
ax.set_xlabel("Month")
ax.set_ylabel("Revenue (€)")
ax.legend(fontsize=10)
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()




Monthly revenue trend across Turbo and Shopify channels (simulated)

title: Predictive Inventory Optimisation Dashboard
text:
Python Code - Seasonal Revenue Heatmap

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

# Simulate seasonal heatmap data (2022–2024)
month_labels = ["Jan","Feb","Mar","Apr","May","Jun",
                "Jul","Aug","Sep","Oct","Nov","Dec"]
years = [2022, 2023, 2024]

np.random.seed(7)
data = np.array([
    [38000 + 8000*np.sin(np.pi*(m-2)/6) + np.random.normal(0, 2000)
     for m in range(12)]
    for _ in years
])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(13, 4))
im = ax.imshow(data, cmap="Blues", aspect="auto")
ax.set_xticks(range(12))
ax.set_xticklabels(month_labels)
ax.set_yticks(range(len(years)))
ax.set_yticklabels(years)
ax.set_title("Seasonal Revenue Heatmap — Turbo Inventory (Simulated)",
             fontweight="bold", pad=12)

for i in range(len(years)):
    for j in range(12):
        ax.text(j, i, f"€{data[i,j]/1000:.0f}k",
                ha="center", va="center", fontsize=7,
                color="white" if data[i,j] > 50000 else "black")

plt.colorbar(im, ax=ax, label="Revenue (€)")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()




Seasonal revenue heatmap showing monthly demand patterns by year (simulated)

title: Predictive Inventory Optimisation Dashboard
text:
Methodology

Qualitative Strand

Semi-structured interviews with CEO, Head of Warehouse Operations, and industry experts
On-site observation and business process mapping of current reorder workflow
Thematic analysis to identify operational requirements and usability needs
PRISMA systematic literature review of state-of-the-art inventory management systems



Quantitative Strand

CRISP-DM framework for structured ML model development
Data sources: Shopify EPOS, Turbo WMS, MINTsoft — SKU-level historical sales
Models under evaluation: ARIMA, Prophet, XGBoost, LSTM
Evaluation metrics: MAE, RMSE, MSE across identical train/test splits







%%{init: {'theme': 'base', 'themeVariables': {'edgeLabelBackground':'#ffffff', 'lineColor':'#1a3a2a', 'arrowheadColor':'#1a3a2a'}}}%%
flowchart LR
  A[Data Collection] --> B[EDA]
  B --> C[Model Training]
  C --> D[Evaluation]
  D --> E[Dashboard]






After model training, forecast accuracy will be evaluated using Mean Absolute Error: \[MAE = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} |y_i - \hat{y}_i|\]

title: Predictive Inventory Optimisation Dashboard
text:
Current Progress

Phase 1 Complete - Client briefing, team formation, project proposal submitted
PRISMA literature review underway - Google Scholar search strategy defined
On-site warehouse visit completed - As-Is process maps drafted
Semi-structured interviews conducted with CEO and warehouse operatives
EDA completed on Turbo (33,213 rows) and Shopify (9,700 rows) datasets
Quantitative model training and comparison (Phase 3 - commencing soon)
Dashboard development and client integration
Final presentation to client and DCU
